request_debug.prompt_preview:
You rerank dead-startup candidates for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and K candidates retrieved by hybrid search, score each candidate from four perspectives, then return ONLY the top 5 ranked from most to least relevant.

Perspectives (each scored 0-10 with a one-sentence rationale):
- business_model: how similar are the revenue, pricing, and customer-relationship shapes?
- market: how similar are the target buyers, market size, and demand drivers?
- gtm: how similar are the go-to-

response.text: {"ranked":[{"candidate_id":"quibi::media_content","perspective_scores":{"business_model":{"score":4,"rationale":"Both are ad-supported (or free-tier) mobile content platforms targeting consumers, but Quibi was subscription-based premium streaming rather than ad-supported social UGC."},"market":{"score":5,"rationale":"Both target mobile-first younger consumers seeking short-form content, but Quibi aimed at a broad national audience rather than a college-community niche."},"gtm":{"score":4,"rationale":"Both relied on mobile app distribution and viral/word-of-mouth growth, but Quibi spent heavily on traditional marketing and studio partnerships rather than organic campus virality."},"stage_scale":{"score":3,"rationale":"Quibi failed at massive VC-backed scale ($1.75B raised) whereas the pitch describes an early-stage college-viral app, making scale at failure very different."}},"rationale":"Quibi shares the short-form mobile content angle and consumer focus, but differs substantially in business model (subscription vs. ad-supported), scale, and GTM motion."},{"candidate_id":"blockbuster::retail_ecommerce","perspective_scores":{"business_model":{"score":1,"rationale":"Blockbuster was a physical/digital video rental and retail business, bearing no resemblance to an ad-supported social voice-note app."},"market":{"score":2,"rationale":"Both serve broad consumer entertainment markets, but Blockbuster targeted general video-rental customers of all ages, not college-age social media users."},"gtm":{"score":1,"rationale":"Blockbuster relied on physical store footprint and later DVD-by-mail, completely unlike a mobile app with campus viral loops."},"stage_scale":{"score":1,"rationale":"Blockbuster failed as a multi-billion-dollar 9,000-store legacy chain, nothing like an early-stage social app."}},"rationale":"Blockbuster is a legacy retail/media company with virtually no overlap in business model, market, GTM, or stage compared to an early-stage ad-supported voice social app."},{"candidate_id":"wikipedia.org","perspective_scores":{"business_model":{"score":1,"rationale":"Pebble was a hardware company selling smartwatches via crowdfunding and retail, with no resemblance to an ad-supported social app model."},"market":{"score":2,"rationale":"Pebble targeted tech-enthusiast consumers broadly, with some college-age overlap, but the buyer profile and demand drivers are fundamentally different."},"gtm":{"score":2,"rationale":"Pebble used Kickstarter crowdfunding and retail channels, completely unlike mobile app viral/college GTM motions."},"stage_scale":{"score":3,"rationale":"Pebble did reach a meaningful consumer scale (~2M units) before failing, which is loosely comparable to a scaling consumer product, but the hardware context makes comparison weak."}},"rationale":"Pebble is a hardware wearables company with almost no structural similarity to an ad-supported social voice app in business model, market, or GTM."}]}
